items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food in a microwave?
items.correct: Radiation — microwaves are electromagnetic radiation that makes polar water molecules vibrate, generating heat.
items.misconception: Microwaves cook food from the inside out.
items.why: Microwaves penetrate ~1–2 cm into food and excite water molecules — they cook outer regions first, conducting heat inward. The 'inside out' myth comes from feeling cool outer surfaces after microwaving (because steam carries heat out).

items.q: Which vitamin is most likely to be deficient in a poorly planned vegan diet?
items.why: Students guess vitamin C (it's the famous one), but plant foods are rich in C. B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products, so vegans need fortified foods or supplements.

items.correct: Acidic conditions (low pH) prevent most bacteria from growing.
items.misconception: The vinegar kills the food cells.
items.why: Students think pickling poisons bacteria. It works by creating an environment INHOSPITABLE to most microbes - low pH stops them growing, even if they're present.

items.q: In the claw grip, knuckles are.
items.why: Correct answer: Stretched forward. “Down on the board” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What stops the chopping board sliding?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Damp cloth. “Tape” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many knife grips are taught (bridge, claw)?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
